problem_statement: When exporting tabular data to CSV, values that contain a bare carriage-return character (one not followed by a line feed) are written into the output without any protection, so reading the output back splits those values across extra rows and the data no longer matches what was exported. Values containing commas, quote characters, or ordinary newlines are handled correctly. Please fix the export path so that affected values survive a write-then-read round trip unchanged.

Percentile summaries computed with this library disagree with numpy and spreadsheet software on the same inputs. For example, the median of the four values 1, 2, 3, 4 comes back as 3 instead of 2.5, and the lower quartile of a five-value series is noticeably too high. The minimum and maximum endpoints are returned correctly, as are single-value series, so only interior percentiles drift. Please correct the calculation so interior percentiles interpolate between order statistics the way the documentation describes.

Grouping and pivoting break on real-world data whenever a grouping key happens to be a string like 'constructor', '__proto__', 'toString', or another name of a built-in object member. Depending on the exact key, processing either throws a TypeError partway through or silently drops entire groups and columns from the result. We also noticed that groups keyed by numeric-looking strings such as '10' come back reordered instead of in first-seen order, which the documentation explicitly promises. Ordinary alphabetic keys behave fine. Please make grouping robust so any string key works and ordering guarantees hold.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: The configurable array-combination behavior of the deep merge utility only takes effect for arrays sitting directly under the top level of the merged objects. Arrays nested any deeper, for example a list under config.tags, are silently replaced wholesale by the source list even when the caller asked for concatenation or union semantics. The default replace behavior looks right at every depth, which is probably why nobody noticed until now. The documentation says the chosen strategy applies no matter how deeply the arrays are nested. Please make the configured behavior apply consistently at every depth.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: Add support for explicit bin edges to the histogram function in the stats module. Extend HistogramOptions with an optional `edges?: number[]` property and honour it in `histogram(values, options)`: when `edges` is provided it takes precedence over `bins`. The edges must contain at least two values and be strictly increasing; otherwise throw a RangeError. Given k edges, return k-1 bins shaped `{ x0, x1, count }` where bin i counts values v with `edges[i] <= v < edges[i+1]`, except the final bin, which also counts values equal to the last edge. Values below the first edge or above the last edge are ignored. The existing equal-width behaviour must remain unchanged whenever `edges` is absent.
notes: Feature task: oracle.patch implements HistogramOptions.edges via a histogramWithEdges helper. | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: CSV data containing multi-line text fields gets corrupted somewhere between writing and reading. Quoted values with embedded line breaks come back split across multiple malformed rows, stray quote characters appear inside restored values, and Windows-style CRLF line breaks within a field are altered even though the value was properly quoted on output. The corruption then propagates into downstream processing: after a write-then-read round trip, grouping the restored records produces phantom extra groups and wrong group counts. Single-line data, quoting of embedded commas, and doubled-quote escaping all still behave correctly, so the problem only surfaces once field values span lines. Please restore the documented round-trip guarantee for multi-line fields.
notes: Cross-module invariant: parse(serialize(x)) identity, symptoms observed through transform.groupBy over round-tripped records. Defect spans the parser state machine and serializer field encoding.

problem_statement: Implement the to_roman(value) function in py/roman.py. It converts an integer to an uppercase Roman numeral using standard subtractive notation (IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM), so 1994 becomes MCMXCIV and 49 becomes XLIX. One twist: zero is representable and returns the medieval nulla symbol "N". Accept only int values from 0 through 3999 inclusive; raise ValueError for floats, negatives, or anything above 3999. Verify with python3 -m pytest -q tests/py/test_roman*.py.

Implement rle_encode(text) in py/rle.py. Scan the string into maximal runs of one character: a run of length one emits just the character, while longer runs emit the decimal count followed by the character (twelve a's give "12a"). Escape rule: when the run's character is itself a digit 0-9 or a backslash, prefix it with a single backslash so decoding stays unambiguous — "1222" encodes to backslash-1, 3, backslash-2. Empty input returns the empty string; non-str input raises TypeError. Verify with python3 -m pytest -q tests/py/test_rle*.py.

Implement merge_intervals(intervals, merge_touching=True) in py/intervals.py. Given a list of closed numeric (start, end) tuples in any order, return a new ascending-sorted list where every group of overlapping intervals is collapsed into one spanning interval. The merge_touching flag controls the boundary policy: when True (default), intervals that merely touch — one ending exactly where the next starts — also merge; when False they stay separate. Raise ValueError if any interval has start greater than end, never modify the input list, and return [] for empty input. Verify with python3 -m pytest -q tests/py/test_intervals*.py.

Implement is_valid_code(code) in py/checksum.py, a Luhn-style check over base-36. Uppercase the code and strip spaces and hyphens first. The result must be at least two characters, all from 0-9 or A-Z; otherwise raise ValueError. Map digits to 0-9 and letters A-Z to 10-35. Walking from the rightmost character, double the value of every second character (2nd, 4th, ... from the right); if a doubled value reaches 36 or more, subtract 35. Sum everything and return True exactly when the total is divisible by 36. Verify with python3 -m pytest -q tests/py/test_checksum*.py.

Implement isBalanced(source) in ts/brackets.ts. Return true when every bracket among (), [], {} and <> is matched and closed in proper LIFO order; all other characters are ignored. Quote-awareness twist: a double quote opens a string literal running to the next unescaped double quote, and everything inside it — brackets included — must be skipped; inside a literal a backslash escapes the following character. If a literal is still open when the input ends, return false. The empty string is balanced. Verify with bun test tests/ts/brackets.

Implement slugify(title) in ts/slug.ts to turn a human-readable title into a URL slug. First remove apostrophes entirely — both ASCII ' and typographic ’ — so their neighbours join ("Don't" becomes "dont"). Then lowercase the text, replace every maximal run of characters outside a-z and 0-9 with a single hyphen, and trim any leading or trailing hyphens. A title containing no alphanumeric characters at all yields the empty string. Verify with bun test tests/ts/slug.

Implement ordinal(n) in ts/ordinal.ts, returning the number followed by its English ordinal suffix as a string. Numbers ending in 1, 2 or 3 take st, nd or rd respectively (1st, 42nd, 63rd) and everything else takes th — except the teens rule: when the last two digits are 11, 12 or 13 the suffix is th (11th, 112th, 1013th). Zero yields "0th". Negative, fractional, NaN or infinite inputs must throw a RangeError. Verify with bun test tests/ts/ordinal.

Implement driftEncode(text, shift) in ts/drift.ts, a Caesar cipher whose shift drifts. The first letter of the text is rotated by shift, the second letter by shift+1, the third by shift+2, and so on, wrapping modulo 26 and preserving case. Non-letter characters are copied through unchanged and do not advance the drift counter. Negative shifts and shifts beyond 26 are legal; a non-integer shift throws a RangeError. For example driftEncode("abc", 0) returns "ace". The empty string maps to itself. Verify with bun test tests/ts/drift.

Implement chunk(items, size, pad) in js/chunk.js. Split the array into consecutive chunks of size elements, returning a new array of new arrays and never modifying the input. size must be an integer of at least 1, otherwise throw a RangeError. When the input length is not a multiple of size, the final chunk stays short — unless pad is provided (any value other than undefined, including null), in which case the final chunk is filled with pad up to exactly size elements. Empty input returns an empty array. Verify with bun test tests/js/chunk.

Implement wordWrap(text, width) in js/wrap.js, returning an array of lines wrapped to the given column width. Words are maximal runs of non-whitespace; whitespace runs are mere separators. Fill greedily: append the next word (joined by one space) while the line stays within width, otherwise start a new line. A word longer than width is hard-broken: flush the current line, emit each full width-sized slice as its own line, and let the final short slice start the next line where later words may join it. No line may exceed width; empty or all-whitespace text returns []. width must be an integer of at least 1, else throw a RangeError. Verify with bun test tests/js/wrap.

Implement parseDuration(spec) in js/duration.js, converting compact duration strings such as "1h30m15s" into total seconds (5415). A spec is one or more components, each being one or more decimal digits followed by a unit h, m or s. Units must appear in strictly descending order, each at most once, though any may be skipped ("1h5s" is 3605). Values are unbounded ("90m" is 5400) and leading zeros are allowed. No whitespace, signs, or other characters may appear, and at least one component is required; any malformed spec throws an Error. Verify with bun test tests/js/duration.

Implement digitalRoot(value, base) in js/droot.js. Write the non-negative integer value in the given base, sum its digits, and repeat on the sum until a single base-digit remains; return that digit's numeric value as a plain number between 0 and base-1. Zero has digital root zero in every base, and any value already below the base is its own root; for example digitalRoot(255, 16) is 15. Throw a RangeError when value is negative or fractional, or when base is not an integer between 2 and 36 inclusive. Verify with bun test tests/js/droot.

When I ask taskman for everything coming up within a given number of days, items that land exactly on the last day of the window never show up. For example, with today pinned to 2026-03-15, a task due 2026-03-22 is missing from `list --due-within 7`, while tasks due earlier in that same week appear fine. Asking for `--due-within 0` also returns nothing even when something is due today. Everything else about the listing (statuses, other filters, sorting) looks right; the window just seems to stop one day short of where it should.
notes: Off-by-one: due_within upper bound became exclusive (< horizon instead of <=).

problem_statement: Tasks added without explicitly picking a priority are being treated as if they were the most urgent kind. After adding a quick note with no priority flag, it jumps to the top of priority-sorted lists and is counted in the most-urgent band of the report breakdown. Our expectation (and long-standing behaviour) is that leaving the priority unspecified gives a middle-of-the-road task that neither jumps queues nor gets buried. Adding tasks with an explicit priority still works exactly as it should; only the unspecified case has gone wrong.
notes: Wrong default: DEFAULT_PRIORITY constant flipped from PRIORITY_MEDIUM to PRIORITY_HIGH.

problem_statement: Free-text search only works when I type the query in exactly the right letter case. Running list with `--search plumber` finds the task titled 'Call plumber about sink', but `--search Plumber` or `--search PLUMBER` come back with an empty table even though the task is clearly there. Searching by tag text has the same problem the moment the query contains an uppercase letter. Search has always been forgiving about case in this tool, and nothing in the help output suggests it should be case-sensitive; please make text matching ignore case again for both titles and tags.
notes: Search needle no longer lower-cased before matching (dropped .lower()).

problem_statement: Filtering by more than one tag has stopped narrowing results. When I run list with `--tag home --tag urgent` I expect only the tasks carrying BOTH tags, but I now get every task that has either one of them — adding more `--tag` flags actually makes the list grow instead of shrink. Filtering by a single tag still behaves correctly, which is probably why nobody noticed for a while. The CLI help for the flag explicitly says 'all given tags must match', and that intersection behaviour is what our saved workflows rely on; combined tag filters should require every requested tag on a task.
notes: filter_tags rewritten from all() (AND) to a first-match loop (OR semantics). | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: The agenda view misfiles tasks that are due exactly one week out. With today pinned at 2026-03-15, a task due 2026-03-22 shows up under the LATER heading, even though the upcoming section is supposed to cover the next seven days inclusive — that last day of the week silently falls out of UPCOMING. Tasks due six days ahead are placed correctly, overdue and today's items are fine, and undated tasks land in UNSCHEDULED as usual, so the whole view is right except for being off by a single day at the window's far edge. This breaks our weekly planning ritual because Sunday's deadline never appears in the coming-week section.
notes: bucket_by_due rewritten around a day-delta; upcoming check drifted to delta < UPCOMING_WINDOW_DAYS (exclusive), so day 7 lands in 'later'. | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: The percentages in the report's priority breakdown no longer add up. On any board that contains completed items the percent column sums to well over 100 — for example, with four tasks of which two are finished we see shares like 50% + 50% + 100%. The raw counts printed right next to them are correct; only the share figures are inflated, and the more work we complete the worse the inflation gets. On a board where everything is completed, every share collapses to 0.0% instead. Each band's percentage should simply be its share of all tasks included in the report, so the column always totals roughly one hundred.
notes: group_by_priority percent denominator became open-tasks-only while counts still cover all tasks. | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: After upgrading taskman, long-time users report that due dates vanished from their oldest task files. Files created by the very first releases still load without any error, and titles, priorities, tags and completion state all come through intact — but every due date comes back empty. The symptoms show up everywhere at once: the list table prints '-' in the DUE column for those tasks, `list --overdue` and `list --due-within` match nothing, the agenda dumps everything into UNSCHEDULED, and the report counts zero overdue items on files that definitely contain late work. Task files written by recent versions are completely unaffected. Worse, once one of these old files has been loaded and saved back, the dates are permanently gone, so this looks like silent data loss on the old-format upgrade path rather than a display problem. Please find where the dates are being dropped and preserve them.
notes: Single root cause in the v1->v2 schema upgrade: record rebuild renames 'due' to 'due_date', and the v2->v3 field whitelist then drops it. Symptoms span list/agenda/report; fix is in migrations only. | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: URGENT — possible data corruption in our shared task database. Since rolling taskman out across three teams (roughly forty task files, several of them synced over network drives), the weekly `report` command shows the overdue counter climbing steadily even though people demonstrably close their tasks on time every single day. We have cross-checked against `list --overdue` on the very same files and the two commands disagree: list shows the one or two genuinely late items, while report claims a dozen or more. The gap grows week over week as more work gets completed, which makes us fear that historical records are being silently corrupted somewhere in the `add`/`done`/`rm` write cycle, or that the JSON files themselves are being damaged during saves and migrations. Compliance dashboards are built directly on these report totals and management reviews them every Monday morning, so we cannot sign off the quarterly audit with numbers we do not trust. Please investigate the persistence and reporting pipeline end to end and restore accurate overdue accounting.
trap: Statement is long, alarmed and enterprise-verbose (corruption fears, many commands, compliance stakes) but the defect is a one-line predicate slip: the report summary counts done tasks as overdue. Router should key on real difficulty, not verbosity.
notes: report.summary counts overdue with an inline predicate that forgets to exclude completed tasks; storage is completely healthy.

problem_statement: Our dashboard currently has to stitch together three separate API calls just to render its little summary card, which is slow and racy. Please add a single overview endpoint to the service: GET /api/stats/overview should behave like the other stats routes with respect to authentication (it must return 401 when no valid token is presented and it requires the read scope) and on success respond 200 with a JSON body of exactly this shape: {"links": <number of stored links>, "notes": <number of stored notes>, "totalClicks": <sum of all recorded link clicks>}. On a completely fresh instance all three numbers should be 0. The data needed already exists in the store; no new bookkeeping should be required.

Short links currently live forever; we need opt-in expiry. POST /api/links should accept an optional ttlSeconds field: when present it must be a positive integer, otherwise the request fails with 400 and body {"error": "invalid_ttl"}. Every link JSON view returned by create, get-by-slug and list must include an expiresAt field, an ISO-8601 timestamp equal to the creation time plus ttlSeconds, or null for links created without a ttl. Redirecting through GET /r/:slug for a link whose expiry has been reached (current time >= expiresAt, boundary inclusive) must answer 410 with {"error": "link_expired"} and must NOT record a click. Metadata reads via GET /api/links/:slug keep returning 200 for expired links so owners can inspect them. All time handling must flow from the app's injected clock.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: Today every API token can read and delete every note, which is unacceptable for shared deployments. Make notes private to the token that created them. POST /api/notes must record the authenticated token's id on the note and include it in the response JSON as an owner field. GET /api/notes must return only notes owned by the calling token, with the existing ?tag= filter applying within that subset. GET /api/notes/:id and DELETE /api/notes/:id must answer 404 when the note exists but belongs to a different token — exactly as if it did not exist, so existence is not revealed — while the owner keeps full access. This applies uniformly to every token, including admin-scoped ones: admin grants no extra note visibility.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: While hardening our client we noticed the service is accepting requests it should refuse. A request that sent the raw token value in the Authorization header with no scheme prefix at all came back 200 with real data, and a colleague who accidentally configured Basic auth with the token as the credential also got through. We even reproduced acceptance with a made-up scheme name in front of a valid token. Properly formed Bearer requests behave exactly as documented, and completely bogus token values are still rejected with 401, so token lookup itself seems fine — it is malformed credential framing that slips past. Please make credential parsing strict again without breaking well-formed requests (including the documented case-insensitive scheme).
notes: ANOMALY 2026-07-06 k=5: cheap(haiku)=0.80 but frontier(sonnet)=0.00 — a real cheap-beats-frontier task; difficulty kept medium pending investigation of sonnet's consistent failure mode.

problem_statement: Our redirect endpoint has started returning 429 to clients that are nowhere near the documented request budget. The clearest repro: a client that gets briefly throttled during one burst then stays throttled indefinitely, even many minutes later when its traffic has completely stopped and the throttling window should long since have rolled over. Worse, clients that never exceed the per-window budget eventually start collecting 429s too if they keep up a slow steady request rate for long enough. Restarting the process clears everything and behaviour inside a single window (first requests admitted, over-budget requests refused with a retry hint) looks exactly right. Please restore the documented recovery behaviour.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: A user created a short link with a custom name yesterday and it silently took over a name a different team had registered months ago: the old destination just vanished and that name now redirects to the new URL. The API even answered 201 as if everything were fine, where we expected the second creation to fail loudly telling the caller the name was taken. After the incident we also noticed the reported total link count no longer agrees with what the list endpoint returns. Generated names still come out unique, and updates or deletes behave normally. Please make creating a link under an already-registered custom name fail with a conflict and leave the existing link fully intact.
notes: RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.

problem_statement: hey, weird one from ops. we renamed a couple of short links last week and the old names sometimes still redirect, and one of them we could not register again even though nothing lists it. after restoring last night's snapshot on the staging box the ghosts were gone, but the renamed link had lost all its click history there. no clean repro yet, sorry — can someone dig into what is going on?
trap: Statement is short and casual while the defect is a cross-module invariant: a slug rename leaves a stale slug-index entry and strands the click ledger under the old key, so live state drifts from what snapshot persistence (which rebuilds the derived index on restore) reproduces — symptoms only align after reasoning across store, persist and stats.
notes: Fix is small (restore index delete + click-ledger migration in the rename path) but diagnosis requires connecting redirect ghosts, list duplicates, slug reuse conflicts and snapshot behaviour divergence. | RE-BINNED 2026-07-06: authored as hard (diagnostic cross-module spread) but mini-calibration k=1 had the cheap arm (haiku) solve it in 46s, and the fix footprint is 8 lines — demoted to medium/mid per the evidence-over-structure rule. Trap flag retained: casual statement, multi-module symptoms. | RE-LABELED 2026-07-06 from k=5 calibration (cheap=haiku 5/5, frontier=sonnet 5/5): no routing signal at authored difficulty — measured reality is cheap-solvable. authored_difficulty preserved.
